ctx.name: Riley
ctx.notes: Riley is in week two and is moving quickly through codebase orientation, but he has mostly learned from docs and async notes rather than direct team contact. I want to check whether he feels clear on ownership boundaries, incident expectations, and where to ask for context before making architecture assumptions. There is a risk he looks productive while building an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. This check-in should identify missing context early and convert it into concrete onboarding support.

focusPoints.label: Ownership boundaries and what “good” means here.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he feels clear on ownership boundaries and expectations for his level, especially around what he should drive vs what he should escalate or coordinate.

focusPoints.label: Where to ask for context before assumptions.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he knows the right places to get team-specific context so he doesn’t build an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates.

focusPoints.label: Incident expectations and escalation norms.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he understands incident expectations and who he should loop in early, so he can act correctly under pressure rather than relying on docs.

focusPoints.label: Concrete onboarding support you can add now.
focusPoints.reason: What he would want more of from you during onboarding—fast feedback loops, clearer decision context, and explicit introductions—so missing context gets filled early instead of later.

transcript.question: What's been most on your mind at work this week, Riley?
transcript.answer: I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.

transcript.question: When you say 'how decisions are really made here,' what feels least clear?
transcript.answer: I can trace most request paths now, though I still miss historical context behind certain tradeoffs.

transcript.question: Which tradeoffs feel hardest to understand the history behind?
transcript.answer: I have not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.

transcript.question: What context are you still missing before you make bigger calls, Riley?
transcript.answer: I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.

transcript.question: That notes doc habit — what makes it hard to ask in the moment?
transcript.answer: I would benefit from a clearer guide for what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.

transcript.question: What would a useful escalate-versus-batch guide need to cover?
transcript.answer: The runbook helped, but some practical handoff details only show up during live incidents.

transcript.question: Who have you had the most useful direct contact with so far, Riley?
transcript.answer: A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster.

transcript.question: What would be most useful from me over the next couple of weeks, Riley?
transcript.answer: If I know exactly what ownership I should take this month, I can contribute with more confidence.

transcript.question: Given what we've covered, what should we tighten up before our next check-in?
transcript.answer: I will ask one architecture question in the next incident review instead of keeping it in my notes doc.

I want to check whether he feels clear on ownership boundaries, incident expectations, and where to ask for context before making architecture assumptions. There is a risk he looks productive while building an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. This check-in should identify missing context early and convert it into concrete onboarding support.
